params.text:
The categories of abuse and neglect

The Care Act statutory guidance lists the main categories of abuse and neglect. The list is not exhaustive, and abuse rarely happens in isolation - a person may experience more than one type at once. Being able to name and recognise these categories helps you spot concerns and describe them clearly when you report.

The categories include physical, domestic, sexual, psychological or emotional, financial or material, modern slavery, discriminatory, organisational, neglect and acts of omission, and self-neglect.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Learn the key categories of abuse and neglect and what each one means.

dialogs.answer: Causing bodily harm - for example hitting, pushing, rough handling, misuse of medication, restraint or inappropriate physical sanctions.
Includes threats, humiliation, controlling behaviour, isolation, intimidation, blaming and verbal abuse that damages a person's wellbeing.
Theft, fraud, pressure over a person's money, wills or property, or misusing their benefits, cash or possessions.
Failing to meet care needs - ignoring medical needs, withholding food, heating or medication, or not providing appropriate care and support.
Poor or harmful care within a service, often from repeated poor practice, rigid routines or a culture that does not put people first.
When a person neglects their own health, hygiene or surroundings, including severe hoarding. Unlike other types, there is no other perpetrator.
